narrative:
As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 38, which edges out the average of 36.70, you brought some strong academic credentials to your law school application. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in the dataset we analyzed. However, the admissions model, trained on thousands of past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 72.5% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure triggered the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass on their first try.

The model's decision hinged most heavily on your school decile rankings from Years 1 and 3, both at the 1st decile—far below the averages of about 5.5-5.7—indicating your high school grades placed you at the bottom of your class. These low rankings were the top two factors pushing the prediction strongly toward bar failure, as they signal potential challenges in sustaining the performance needed for the bar. Your undergraduate GPA, while a positive note above average, played a counterbalancing role as the third most influential factor, nudging the prediction somewhat toward passing but not enough to offset the earlier weaknesses.

Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your high school performance stands out as unusually low, which the model sees as the biggest red flag despite your later improvements in college. Many successful bar takers had mid-level high school deciles like the 5th or 6th, giving them a stronger foundation. To move forward, focus on bolstering your profile for other schools—perhaps by retaking the LSAT for an even higher score, gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your college turnaround, or pursuing additional certifications or work experience in law-related fields to demonstrate resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift future predictions in your favor, showing growth beyond those early high school struggles. Keep pushing; your upward trajectory is already a great sign.
